3.1 Work plan  
Our AI-CUBE project will be implemented through 3 key project phases:  
  
1. Phase 1: Set-Up - Stakeholder engagement strategy, MLA methodology and initial technology assessment  
2. Phase 2: Gap analysis - AI-CUBE business cases and gap analysis vs. current STATUS  
3. Phase 3: Roadmapping - translate gap analysis into skills, data and RD&I requirements per SPIRE process industry sector  
4. Phase 4: Take-up - Disseminate and promote the AI-CUBE roadmaps towards the wider process industries stakeholder communities  
The activities of our project will be structured in 6 work packages (WPs) as shown in fig. 16.  
  
Figure 16. PERT Chart  
  
In the set-up phase 1 of our project, WP1 will start with an extensive literature and stakeholder analysis, to establish the current technological and industrial landscape regarding AI and BD technologies and process industry sectors in Europe, setting the basis of the following WPs work in defining mapping tools and the roadmap for AI and BD. In parallel, in WP2 a stakeholder engagement strategy will be set-up based on the MAMCA methodology, to work in a continuous way with the stakeholders and to design a framework to assess and categorize current Maturity Levels of AI and BD technologies’ applications and deployment. Phase 2 of our project will be targeted at carrying out a gap-analysis. First, WP3 will assess the maturity level of the previously identified technologies, while WP4 will define AI and BD driven Business Cases and Business Models for the process industries of the future. In WP4 a mapping of such visionary Business Cases and Models against the current STATUS (WP3) will be carried out to establish gaps and development needs per SPIRE process industry sector and per macro-application process areas (as defined in the CUBE). In the roadmapping phase 3, results from the previous phases will be translated into development needs and recommendations in terms of skills, data and RD&I requirements. As such WP4 will define a roadmap on AI and BD application in process industries for all the SPIRE industrial sectors. WP5 and WP6 will be horizontal to the whole project, focusing on dissemination and project coordination respectively, and culminating in the last Take-up phase 4, where the results of our project (the CUBEs, MLA framework, learnings and roadmaps) will be promoted towards the wider process industries communities (starting with > 100 industrial parks already identified in the previous INSPIRE project) and working in close collaboration with A.SPIRE.  
Based on previous experience with SPIRE and FoF CSA’s, our AI-CUBE consortium aims for an early involvement of A.SPIRE in the project, already discussed with the A.SPIRE office. This will be leveraged to steer our project towards optimal and usable results, that can be taken-up by the eight (plus possibly pulp and paper, glass, etc.) process industries, as they come available in the course of our project.

3.1 Work plan — Work packages, deliverables  
In line with the technological objectives that we have to overcome in this 48-month project, the work to be accomplished has been structured with the following work packages (WP) (Figure 3.1), and each WP has been sub-divided into a number of tasks, which are listed in detail in Figure 3.2 (Gantt chart). Each WP will be led and managed by a project partner who is an expert in the relevant field. The WP structure aims to set the basis for new bio-based value chains with a focus on creating new cross-sector interconnections in bio-based economy clusters. The objectives will address issues raised by the NMBP-04-2020 topic and will be implemented in a reasonable and cost-effective manner. WP objectives are built on integrated production methods, delivery of added value polymers, cost-effective accurate characterizations, and variability of product evaluation through LCA approach. We will generate knowledge, develop novel bioprocessing technologies, and industrial concepts transferrable to upscale testing. All 35 partners will collaborate in order to successfully deliver and achieve the BIOMAC objectives.

3.1 Work plan — Work packages, deliverables and milestones  
3.1.1 Overall structure and Pert Chart  
A competitive work plan has been devised in order to deliver on the objectives of our CEM-WAVE project to ensure that we fulfill our goals and vision to catalyse the activities to produce novel materials capable of resisting fluctuation and extreme future conditions and to provide the requirements of our CEM-WAVE use case. KPIs have been used as the starting point for planning the work that needs to be implemented within our project. All the actions that are required to meet the objectives of our Research and Innovation Action have been collectively analysed by our interdisciplinary consortium. We agreed on how best to distribute the workload among the various experts by making best use of their relevant fields of knowledge, capacity, infrastructure, and resources. This bottom-up planning process has greatly facilitated our task of arriving at a clear, comprehensive, and realistic work plan (see Gantt Chart and Pert Diagram below), whereby all partners have ownership of our work plan and its approach. Of a total of 10 WPs aimed at accomplishing the broad scope of this SPIRE topic as previously justified, our R&D work is split into 6 WPs, connected with the one in which a use case will be analyzed, each with clear tasks that will enable our project’s objectives to be fulfilled through a coherent structure that will also facilitate project management (WP10), sustainability study (WP8), and the further dissemination and exploitation of the results (WP9).  

Stakeholder CEM-WAVE Benefits and Communication strategy

The first research phase (WP1 and WP2) deals with the preparation of the fiber coating and improves our knowledge of the solid-state MW technology. The heart of CEM-WAVE is characterized by WP3, in which oxide and non-oxide CMCs will be produced with the implemented MW-CVI technique. All around there are WPs of support about the WP3 activity: AI-assisted modeling (WP5), Characterization also in relevant environments (WP4), and joining R&D on CMCs (WP6). Concurrently, the CMC gradually designed, produced, and joined will be tested by our CEM-WAVE use case work package (WP7). As described before, our work plan is encased in a robust environmental and thermoeconomical analysis (WP8), in a fundamental project exploitation, dissemination, and communication plan (WP9), and in a well-established and efficient project management structure and procedures (WP10). Our work plan is linked to the objectives set out in Section 1.1, and we are confident that this work plan is robust to ensure that our project’s S&T and innovation goals are fulfilled.

Objectives  
- Development of oxide and non-oxide preforms to be processed in WP3 by microwave CVI assisted densification. Moreover, the preform will be used for joining experiments in WP6  
- Different fibre preform architectures will be required. The preform thickness will be between 3 mm to 9 mm  
- Different fibre lay-up architectures and fibre orientations will be investigated to achieve an appropriate porosity  
- For the primary stabilization of the fibre preforms, binder systems will be developed  
- Total porosity and the pore size distribution of the preforms will need to be adjusted  

Description of work  
Firstly, we will define the technical and specific requirements for applications of both oxide and non-oxide CMCs in more detail by AM. This includes especially the sample geometries and maximum service temperature. At the beginning of the process chain for the CMC production, both oxide and non-oxide ceramic fibres need to be coated to adjust the interfacial domain between fibre and matrix for the production of Weak Interface Composites, WICs (Task 1.1 & Task 1.2), e.g., non-oxide and oxide CMCs with low matrix porosity. Note that uncoated preforms will be required for the Weak Matrix Composites, WMCs, e.g., for the oxide composites with high matrix porosity. At FHF, we will coat ceramic fibres continuously in a roll-to-roll process by a wet-chemical route. The technology can provide coated fibres in sufficient quantity and in a reasonable time. The coated fibres will be delivered to Task 1.3, where we will develop preforms with selected geometries by techniques such as filament winding or prepreg technology based on 2D fabrics. Three manufacturing loops will be needed to achieve the most suitable preform quality. Commercially available SiC fibres like 2nd or 3rd generation SiC fibres (e.g., UBE-ZMI, UBE-Tyranno SA, NGS-Hi Nicalon, NGS Hi-Nicalon type S) as well as oxide fibres (e.g., 3M-Nextel 610 & 720) will be necessary to allow manufacturing and application temperatures up to 1200°C. The preforms can either be infiltrated first by MW-CVI or be joined first and then infiltrated.  

Task 1.1 Coating set-up of non-oxide fibres for CMC's production (M1-10)  
Non-oxide ceramic fibres will be coated in a continuous roll-to-roll process at FHF. We will optimize the coating parameters to achieve homogeneous coatings with a high degree of coverage and minimized fibre bridging. 3.1 Work plan and resources Brief presentation of the overall structure of our work plan Our project workplan has been designed to allow us, over the course of 4 years, and in an application-oriented approach to develop, test, optimize, scale-up and validate (from TLR3 to TRL6), the following innovative and scalable technologies that are -by- -by- - pharmaceutical manufacture (and that are also less environmentally harmful during use and disposal): HME (hot melt extrusion); Discontinuous Synthesis (batch reaction) using Isobutyl and acetone; Rapid microfluidic mixing combined with Diafiltration; Modular flow reactors continuous systems; Genome engineering for strain optimization, bioprocessing intensification and microjet reactor technology (MJR) for downstreaming processes; Membrane purification. WP1 (Green innovation in pharmaceutical manufacturing) focuses on the R&D phase to bring the technologies from TRL3-4, and WP3 (Industry cocreation lab: Pilots) will further scale up to TRL5-6. WP2 (Digitalisation for competitive & scalable methods of production) will develop digital technologies (digital work flows for chemical data, PAT and Digital Twins) to facilitate the process develop and understanding. In parallel, WP4 (Environmental assessment & ecotoxicity studies) will, in addition to performing environmental risk and life cycle analysis within our project case studies, will also bridge the current knowledge gaps in relation to the environmental concentration and resulting levels of risk of pharmaceuticals already in use, and in particular, building knowledge of the impact of molecules on the environment through the eco-toxicity studies for improved management of environmental risk and for designing of new molecules. From a multi-stakeholder and a social innovation perspective, WP5 (Communication, Dissemination & Change labs), will ensure we engage decision-makers from the wider pharmaceutical industry, public authorities, healthcare practitioners as well as citizens themselves towards an integrated movement behind catalysing not only the adoption of green pharmaceutical technologies, but also promoting an enabling regulatory environment, as well as paving the way forward for changing social norms for safe consumption and disposal of pharmaceuticals (and also considering gendered innovation aspects). Finally, WP6 (Project Management and Pathway to impact) will ensure we encase our project in a robust and comprehensive project management framework, that in addition to project monitoring, tracking and risk management, also encompasses data management, management of Open Science, Standardization activities, as well as preparation for the post-project exploitation phase towards further scale up industrial deployment of the results, and ensuring wide adoption across the EU of the ETERNAL outputs for environmental and competitiveness impacts across the European pharmaceutical industry. To develop greener nanoparticle manufacturing methods that provide nano formulations of the same quality as classic methods. WP1 is linked to Task 2..6 Compliance by design. Description of work (where appropriate, broken down into tasks), lead partner and role of participants Task 1.1.1. Preliminary evaluation & status quo on HME. We will perform a review of the state of the art with the intention to apply this technology to more pharmaceutical products owned by Angelini (to go beyond the case study developed). Chemical-physical properties of the compounds will be investigated to verify suitability for HME technology. Current manufacturing processes for medicines that contain Ibuprofen and Trazodone will be analysed in depth for changing to continuous manufacturing (HME). In addition to the study on the active ingredient, we will carry out research activities on polymers, excipients and plasticizers potentially usable in extrusion processes. Task 1.1.2 Preformulating trials and formulation trials: We will perform first laboratory tests on Ibuprofen and Trazodone: Compatibility studies (API and excipients); Thermal characterization of API: polymer: plasticizer mixtures; Extrusion trials of different preliminary mixtures (DoE based on screw configuration, screw speed, throughput and temperature profile); Chemical-physical analyses on preliminary extrudates; Definition of the composition; Stress tests; Trials of tablet compression or filling in capsules; 12 month prestability study. Task 1.1.3. Analytical development & stability studies: We will develop new pharmaceutical processes regarding new formulations, dedicated analytical support (both physical and chemical) focused on trials and analysis is needed. Indeed, analytical data on the quality and properties of the product are necessary to gain the detailed process knowledge. Several instruments such as HPLC, Dissolution apparatus, X-ray diffractometer, will be used for this study. Main analytical activities include Analytical development; In parallel, we will develop analytical methods, depending on product specifications. The analytical procedures will provide an acceptable accuracy & precision over the specified range; Raw materials, half-processed and finished products will be analysed and selected prototypes will also be investigated using a stress test protocol; Stability-At the end of the previous studies, we will perform 12 months prestability of drug product at ICH conditions. This task will focus on the synthesis of trazodone (antidepressant API) T1.2.1. Recycling Solvents: the synthesis of trazodone implies the use of 2k tons of solvents per year. The solvents used in the process are acetone and isobutyl alcohol. To improve the current system for the distillation and to capture impurities and reagents (we will follow the ICH quality guidelines), we will carry out the following steps: Preliminary in silico assessments and simulations of the distillation; Laboratory trials (using equipment and pilot/real mixtures): considering previous simulations, we will implement at laboratory scale the better combination of filters to separate both solvents and to capture the impurities (including Chitosan for capturing impurities). Together with BOKU, we will carry out the degradation of those substances using enzymatic steps. The quality of purified solvents will be evaluated by Angelini by gas chromatography (GC) optimizing the filtering sequence if it is needed. T1.2.2. Greener alternatives: We will carry out the synthesis of trazodone at laboratory scale using more sustainable alternatives. We will study biobased solvents (e.g. ethyl lactate, to replace acetone, 2-methyltetrahydrofuran (2-MeTHF) to replace isobutyl). Biobased alternatives, e.g. acetone could be supplied by Caldic -produced through fermentation of sugars from renewable resources, and free of all carcinogenic aromatic impurities (e.g. benzene) that are commonly found in traditionally sourced materials. Trials will be done at AIMPLAS facilities & we will oversee quality evaluation of obtained trazodone (HPLC analysis). Task 1.2. Case Study II (Angelini Pharma): Better use of solvents (recycled and Greener alternatives) (Leader: Angelini Pharma Participants: AIMPLAS, BOKU). Task 1.1 Case Study I-(Angelini Pharma): Continuous manufacturing method-Reduction in energy consumption (Leader: Angelini Pharma Participants: AIMPLAS, EE). ETERNAL (HORIZON-HLTH-2021-IND-07-01) Page 33 of 45 Task 1.3 Case Study III (RJ): Better use of solvents (reduction or elimination). (Leader: Reig Jofre Participants: AIMPLAS, BOKU, UAVR). RJF will oversee developing greener methods to produce Liposomes at laboratory scale. Using liposomes, the bioavailability of API is achieved. This results in a reduction of the required dose to achieve the same pharmacological effect. Reducing the dose and increasing bioavailability increases drug absorption and reduces excretion from the gastrointestinal tract. On a large scale this leads to a reduction of metabolites excreted into the environment. One of the objectives of this task is to increase the encapsulation efficiency of the API, selected, valproic acid and other hydrophilic drugs. The increase of encapsulation efficiency could be achieved by the proper selection of formulation excipients, optimization of process parameters and the selection of more advanced manufacturing methods. 1. Explanation of the work carried out by us and Overview of the progress  
The main objective of POLYBIOSKIN is to formulate and develop new bio-based, biodegradable and biocompatible materials with the main purpose of delivering skin contact products for hygiene, cosmetic and biomedical sector. The products we use as models are a diaper/femcare pad, a beauty mask and a wound dressing. Due to the attention that must be given to these special products that go in contact with delicate parts of the human body and to the huge market they cover, most of the commercializing companies have not paid yet so much attention to the environmental impact these products can cause. In the market, just a few examples claiming the use of biodegradable and/or biobased products are present, but many times no certified tests have been produced (or there is no track of them) and anyway, the performances are still far from the traditional fossil-based counterparts.  

POLYBIOSKIN, during these 3 years of project implementation, tried to solve these issues tackling the following activities:  
• The product formulations have been set up by considering biodegradable/compostable and renewable families of polymers, such as polyesters (PLA and PHAs) or polysaccharides (i.e. pullulan and chitin derivatives)  
• When needed, the additives were added to the formulation after a careful screening taking into account the above mentioned criteria  
• The formulation has also been contrasted and optimized following the industrial requirements and industrial needs for the further scale-up  
• Thanks to the dissemination activities, POLYBIOSKIN was present and advertised in the crucial events with oral, scientific and training activities  
• Following the 1RP review assessment, POLYBIOSKIN also described the regulatory framework of the sectors of application, paving the way for the acceleration of the market uptake.  

As main outcomes, in line with the contractual statement we report the fact sheets of the three Compostable Bio-based Baby Diaper with design and performance in line with current fossil-based Baby diapers. The perforated 3D topsheet (the element in contact with baby skin) was developed by Texol in collaboration with INSTM (compostable and renewable plastic granule formulation). INSTM showed potential skin benefits by using human keratinocytes and stem cells. Moreover, the natural immune system of skin (defensins) is enhanced, so a natural anti-microbial action is obtained. The absorbent core (cotton or cellulose fibers + Bio superabsorbent particles) was developed by Fibroline using a proprietary impregnation technology. The impregnation technology uses oscillating electric fields and does not require latex or glue.

The complexity of immune reactions explicates via the production of both pro- and anti-inflammatory cytokines and antibacterial molecules with different time scales, which ultimately ensure an efficient tissue response to foreign agents. (WP4 and WP7; milestones: MS7-12). Work carried out towards its achievement A platform of nanostructured complexes based on CN-LG was investigated in POLYBIOSKIN. These complexes incorporated biobased molecules like GA, vitamin C, vitamin E, lutein, niacinamide. We characterized and selected these complexes on the basis of their compatibility with skin, thermal resistance and specific properties for the different applications. It is important to point out that, although these components are nanostructured, they are micro-sized due to the complexation with biopolymers, lignin fragments or inorganic materials. Singularly, these nanomaterials are biodegradable in the human body by natural enzymes, thus they do not constitute a source of accumulation in organs and tissues. These intermediaries, that can give access to highly transversal and widely applicable bionanotechnologies, are fundamental to add additional properties, such as a greater thermal stability, light and oxygen stability, controlled and targeted release of functional biomolecules, enhanced water solubility and also the capacity of avoiding the agglomeration of biomolecules. This topic was the subject of a scientific publication11. Regarding the acceptance of bionanotechnologies, we developed beauty masks that underwent an in vivo study with 30 human volunteers. Results based on antiaging and overall skin compatibility were validated. Consumer acceptance was positive and as for the dermatological surveillance, our beauty mask showed good skin compatibility properties, claiming as “Dermatologically tested”, “Clinically Tested” and “Tolerance Tested”. Description of the work carried out Overview of the work carried out (UOS) This work package was used to lay the foundation of our project. The main bio-polymeric materials and the active biomolecules to be used for each application were finalised. Production and characterisation of the biomaterials, PHAs, superabsorbent material (bio-SAP) and biomolecules including CN and CN-LG complexes were successfully achieved. Task 1.1 Specifications of POLYBIOSKIN applications (EUBP, INSTM, all) A Public Report on the specifications of the biopolymers and bio-additives for the different applications was produced as an outcome of this Task. The report presented a detailed overview over all the key materials and selected additives that we used in POLYBIOSKIN, including the technical biopolymers PLA, PBAT, PBS, and PHAs, the polysaccharides including pullulan, starch, cellulose, chitin and chitosan, and naturally occurring polymers such as lignin and gelatine, as well as PEG. The report detailed the key characteristics of these selected materials, e.g. sourcing (production/synthesis), biodegradability, biocompatibility, technical characteristics, current uses, and includes commercial and sustainability aspects. Commercial competitiveness of our POLYBIOSKIN biopolymeric materials and target applications has shown no fundamental barriers to commercial exploitation. The main challenge was to increase the interaction between the hydrophobic polymer and hydrophilic active components. Various physical and chemical methods were explored. • Physical Methods: 1. Preparing water-borne CN-LG complexes using PLA oligomer (for diaper/beauty mask and wound dressing applications) 2. Plasma treatment was applied on two medium chain length PHA films resulting in increased hydrophilicity. • Chemical Methods: 1. Incorporation of chemically modified CN-LG via “grafting from” method by PLA oligomer in bulk (PLA) (in diaper and beauty mask) 2. Incorporation of chemically modified CN-LN via “grafting from” method by PLA oligomer by coatings (in diaper and beauty mask) 3. PHA treatment with NaOH, HCl and H2SO4 and introduction of –OH and –COOH groups in order to increase hydrophilicity of the polymer (in wound dressing). Overall the methods developed led to more hydrophilic surfaces of the biopolymeric materials required for their efficient use in the range of applications. 3.1 Work plan and resources Brief presentation of the overall structure of the work plan: Our work plan is organised into 8 technical WPs, 3 innovation-impact WPs, and 3 Project Management WPs, structured across three development phases aligned with the progression from TRL4–5 to TRL6. Phase 1 (WP1 –WP3, TRL4–5) will establish the foundations for circular NIPU materials, covering circular -by- design chemistry, structure –property–process relationships, and a QbD -driven digital framework including FAIR data and digital twin architecture. This phase defines our design space across key material configurations and aligns performance, processability and recyclability from the outset. Phase 2 (WP4–WP6, TRL5) will focus on manufacturing integration and circularity validation. It includes pilot-scale processing using PU-compatible technologies, development of recycling and depolymerisation routes, and deployment of digital twin, FAIR data and DPP systems for traceability. Upstream sorting of NIPU-relevant waste streams enables our integration of secondary feedstocks into the value chain. Phase 3 (WP7–WP8, TRL6) will deliver industrial demonstrators and system-level validation. WP7 develops and validates TRL6 mobility components, including functional and regulatory testing, while WP8 performs environmental, techno-economic and circular value-chain assessments, defining pathways for industrial uptake. WP9–WP11 covers our dissemination, exploitation, standardisation, training and business case development. WP12–WP14 ensures our project management, coordination, quality and risk management across the consortium. This structure is suited to a Lump Sum funding model, in that WPs are contained across three reporting periods (RP1: M1-M18; RP2: M19 -M36; RP3: M37 -M48, following the Horizon Europe Guide for Proposers for Lump Sum Grants29. Refer to figure 2 for a graphical presentation of our work structure components showing how they inter-relate. 3.1 Work plan and resources Overall Strategy of the work plan: Our CO2HESION work plan has been designed based on our project objectives and consists of 13 interlinked Work Packages (WPs) that support the development, integration, and validation of our CO2HESION platform, as illustrated in the PERT diagram (Figure 7), which defines the logical sequencing, dependencies, and feedback loops between the WPs. Our work plan will start with the definition of the industrial context and system requirements in WP1 (Industrial process and cluster specification), which will establish the boundary conditions, use cases, and reference scenarios for our project. This provides the basis for the development of core materials and components in WP 2 (Material Development for CO2 capture resilience and utilisation), followed by their validation at laboratory scale in WP 3 (Lab-scale validation of CO2 Capture and Utilisation systems). Building on these results, we will move to system-level implementation through WP4 (Pilot Plant Design and Construction), where our integrated platform is engineered and assembled. This is followed by WP5 (Process Demonstration under Dynamic/Renewable Loading), which validates our system under industrially relevant and dynamic operating conditions, including variable energy inputs and real process constraints. In parallel, WP6 (Digital Twin, System Modelling and Industrial Upscaling) provides the system-level modelling and digital infrastructure required to support monitoring, analysis, and optimisation of our integrated platform, as well as the assessment of scale-up scenarios. Complementary to this, WP7 (Sustainability, Techno-Economic, and Environmental Assessment) evaluates the performance of our system in terms of cost, environmental impact, and industrial feasibility. Our project impact will be consolidated through a comprehensive series of activities in WP8 –WP10 that will cover communication, dissemination, exploitation, and standardisation across the three reporting periods, ensuring continuity and progressive development of impact-related actions. Coordination and governance are ensured through WP11-13 ensuring effective project management across the full duration of our project. Description of work T1.1: Specification and Boundaries of Industrial Scale Emissions. Lead: TOR; Participants: ORL, UNIZAR, INEGI, TRSA, ACC; M1-M12 Our objective of Task 1.1 is to define the physical and chemical design basis for our integrated CCU platform by characterizing the real-world emission profiles of the host industrial sites. TOR, ACC and ORL will provide high-resolution primary data on their respective industrial CO2 streams, encompassing mass flow rates, temperature fluctuations, and detailed chemical compositions. A critical focus will be placed on identifying and quantifying trace impurities, such as SO x, NOx, moisture content, and particulate matter, which serve as essential inputs for the catalyst durability studies in WP2 and WP3 and the pilot engineering in WP4. Beyond gas composition, this task will perform a comprehensive thermal mapping of the industrial sites to identify available low-grade and high-grade waste heat sources. This data is fundamental to our project's energy-positive concept, as it allows for the integration of waste heat into the CCU process units, significantly reducing the external energy demand. Furthermore, our industrial partners will provide data on local renewable energy availability and grid constraints. The resulting multi-dimensional dataset will be used to create the synthetic gas mixtures for laboratory-scale validation and will provide the boundary conditions for the WP6 system models and the final engineering scale-up, ensuring our platform is designed for the specific dynamic stresses of the ceramic and petrochemical sectors. 3.1 Work plan and resources Brief presentation of the overall structure of the work plan: Our work plan is organised into 8 technical WPs, 3 innovation-impact WPs, and 3 Project Management WPs, structured across three development phases aligned with our progression from TRL4–5 to TRL6. Phase 1 (WP1 –WP3, TRL4–5) will establish the foundations for circular NIPU materials, covering circular-by-design chemistry, structure–property–process relationships, and a QbD-driven digital framework including FAIR data and digital twin architecture. This phase defines our design space across key material configurations and aligns performance, processability, and recyclability from the outset. Phase 2 (WP4–WP6, TRL5) will focus on manufacturing integration and circularity validation. It includes pilot-scale processing using PU-compatible technologies, development of recycling and depolymerisation routes, and deployment of digital twin, FAIR data, and DPP systems for traceability. Upstream sorting of NIPU-relevant waste streams enables us to integrate secondary feedstocks into the value chain. Phase 3 (WP7–WP8, TRL6) will deliver industrial demonstrators and system-level validation. WP7 develops and validates TRL6 mobility components, including functional and regulatory testing, while WP8 performs environmental, techno-economic, and circular value-chain assessments, defining pathways for industrial uptake. WP9–WP11 covers our dissemination, exploitation, standardisation, training, and business case development. WP12–WP14 ensures our project management, coordination, quality, and risk management across the consortium. This structure is suited to a Lump Sum funding model, in that WPs are contained across three reporting periods (RP1: M1-M18; RP2: M19-M36; RP3: M37-M48, following the Horizon Europe Guide for Proposers for Lump Sum Grants29. 3.1 Work plan and resources Brief presentation of the overall structure of the work plan: Our 48-month work plan is structured in 16 work packages (WPs) that advance circular vitrimer composite technology from laboratory proof-of-concept (TRL 3→4) through optimised processes (TRL 5) to validated full-scale construction demonstrators (TRL 6). We implement a phased concurrent-engineering strategy, designed according to the EC’s MOSAIC HORIZON-CL4-2025-05-MATERIALS-42-two-stage guidance for Lump Sum funding modalities to ensure that three reporting periods each contain a cluster of technical, horizontal, and management WPs whose outputs feed directly into the subsequent phase. Phase 1 –Foundation (RP1, M1 -M18, TRL 3 →4). Three technical WPs establish our scientific and technological foundations. WP1 develops our vitrimer material platform based on UP/VE dual dynamic covalent chemistry, delivering two optimised formulation families with integrated flame retardancy and SSbD-screened compositions. WP2 creates our smart manufacturing baseline by calibrating pultrusion and LCM process lines, developing embedded resistive wire sensors and thermal actuation hardware, and conducting initial vitrimer processability trials. WP3 builds our digital infrastructure – defining the multi-agent DT architecture, generating baseline durability datasets, developing initial physics-based models, and implementing the sensor data interface. In parallel, WP8 establishes our SSbD framework, WP11 launches our DEC programme including the first citizen co-creation workshop, and WP14 initiates our project management. Phase 2–Optimisation (RP2, M19-M36, TRL 5). WP4 advances our vitrimer formulations to industrial processing windows and pilots scale-up to batches of ≥10kg. WP5 integrates our sensor networks into optimised manufacturing processes, validates reversible adhesive bonding/debonding protocols, and pre-stages smart components for demonstrator assembly. WP6 builds our comprehensive multi-scale vitrimer durability database and trains PINNs and prescriptive AI models within the DT. WP9 continues our SSbD compliance monitoring, WP12 intensifies our DEC activities, and WP15 maintains our coordination. Phase 3 –Validation (RP3, M37 -M48, TRL 6). WP7 produces our three full-scale circular construction demonstrators, validates prescriptive intelligence and self-healing under representative service conditions, and quantifies circularity performance through chemical recycling (fibre recovery >96%), 6R-framework assessment (≥30% circularity improvement vs. conventional FRPCs), and DPP implementation. WP10 completes our lifecycle SSbD assessment. WP13 delivers our final DEC impact including the business case, CEN Workshop Agreement contributions, and societal acceptance assessment (target: >75% acceptance rate among ≥150 engaged citizens). WP16 ensures our final reporting and governance closure. The PERT diagram (Figure 3) shows the graphical presentation of our WP inter-dependencies. Structured around 3 parallel and interdependent pipelines that converge at the demonstrator stage (WP7), our materials pipeline develops vitrimer formulations from lab-scale design to industrial readiness (WP1 –WP4), the manufacturing pipeline integrates sensing systems and produces smart components (WP2 –WP5), and the digital pipeline builds the DT and prescriptive intelligence framework (WP3 –WP6). These pipelines have defined handover points and progress in parallel to ensure continuous data exchange and alignment. Their convergence in WP7 enables the integrated validation of materials, manufacturing, and digital intelligence in full-scale demonstrators. Horizontal WPs on SSbD, sustainability, dissemination and exploitation (WP8–WP13) provide continuous feedback & ensure alignment with safety, circularity and market uptake objectives. 3.1 Work plan and resources Overall Strategy of the work plan: Our CO2HESION work plan has been designed based on our project objectives and consists of 13 interlinked Work Packages (WPs) that support the development, integration, and validation of our CO2HESION platform, as illustrated in the PERT diagram (Figure 7), which defines the logical sequencing, dependencies, and feedback loops between the WPs. This provides the basis for the development of core materials and components in WP 2 (Material Development for CO2 capture resilience and utilisation), followed by their validation at laboratory scale in WP 3 (Lab-scale validation of CO2 Capture and Utilisation systems). This is followed by WP5 (Process Demonstration under Dynamic/Renewable Loading), which validates our system under industrially relevant and dynamic operating conditions, including variable energy inputs and real process constraints. In parallel, WP6 (Digital Twin, System Modelling and Industrial Upscaling) provides the system-level modelling and digital infrastructure required to support monitoring, analysis, and optimisation of our integrated platform, as well as the assessment of scale-up scenarios. Complementary to this, WP7 (Sustainability, Techno-Economic, and Environmental Assessment) evaluates the performance of our system in terms of cost, environmental impact, and industrial feasibility. Our project impact will be consolidated through a comprehensive series of activities in WP8 –WP10 that will cover communication, dissemination, exploitation, and standardisation across the three reporting periods, ensuring continuity and progressive development of impact-related actions. Coordination and governance are ensured through WP11-13, ensuring effective project management across the full duration of our project. 3.1 Work plan and resources Brief presentation of the overall structure of the work plan: Our 48-month work plan is structured in 16 work packages (WPs) that advance circular vitrimer composite technology from laboratory proof-of-concept (TRL 3→4) through optimised processes (TRL 5) to validated full-scale construction demonstrators (TRL 6). We implement a phased concurrent-engineering strategy, designed according to the EC’s MOSAIC HORIZON-CL4-2025-05-MATERIALS-42-two-stage guidance for Lump Sum funding modalities to ensure that three reporting periods each contain a cluster of technical, horizontal, and management WPs whose outputs feed directly into the subsequent phase. Phase 1 –Foundation (RP1, M1 -M18, TRL 3 →4). Three technical WPs establish our scientific and technological foundations. WP1 develops our vitrimer material platform based on UP/VE dual dynamic covalent chemistry, delivering two optimised formulation families with integrated flame retardancy and SSbD-screened compositions. WP2 creates our smart manufacturing baseline by calibrating pultrusion and LCM process lines, developing embedded resistive wire sensors and thermal actuation hardware, and conducting initial vitrimer processability trials. WP3 builds our digital infrastructure – defining the multi-agent DT architecture, generating baseline durability datasets, developing initial physics-based models, and implementing the sensor data interface. In parallel, WP8 establishes our SSbD framework, WP11 launches our DEC programme including the first citizen co-creation workshop, and WP14 initiates our project management. Phase 2–Optimisation (RP2, M19-M36, TRL 5). WP4 advances our vitrimer formulations to industrial processing windows and pilots scale-up to batches of ≥10kg. WP5 integrates our sensor networks into optimised manufacturing processes, validates reversible adhesive bonding/debonding protocols, and pre-stages smart components for demonstrator assembly. WP6 builds our comprehensive multi-scale vitrimer durability database and trains PINNs and prescriptive AI models within the DT. WP9 continues our SSbD compliance monitoring, WP12 intensifies our DEC activities, and WP15 maintains our coordination. Phase 3 –Validation (RP3, M37 -M48, TRL 6). WP7 produces our three full-scale circular construction demonstrators, validates prescriptive intelligence and self-healing under representative service conditions, and quantifies circularity performance through chemical recycling (fibre recovery >96%), 6R-framework assessment (≥30% circularity improvement vs. conventional FRPCs), and DPP implementation. WP10 completes our lifecycle SSbD assessment. WP13 delivers our final DEC impact including the business case, CEN Workshop Agreement contributions, and societal acceptance assessment (target: >75% acceptance rate among ≥150 engaged citizens). WP16 ensures our final reporting and governance closure. The PERT diagram (Figure 3) shows the graphical presentation of our WP inter-dependencies. Structured around 3 parallel and interdependent pipelines that converge at the demonstrator stage (WP7), our materials pipeline develops vitrimer formulations from lab-scale design to industrial readiness (WP1 –WP4), the manufacturing pipeline integrates sensing systems and produces smart components (WP2 –WP5), and the digital pipeline builds the DT and prescriptive intelligence framework (WP3 –WP6). These pipelines have defined handover points and progress in parallel to ensure continuous data exchange and alignment. Their convergence in WP7 enables the integrated validation of materials, manufacturing, and digital intelligence in full-scale demonstrators. Horizontal WPs on SSbD, sustainability, dissemination and exploitation (WP8–WP13) provide continuous feedback & ensure alignment with safety, circularity and market uptake objectives. 3.1 Work plan and resources  
We are organised into 12 work packages that, together, implement the project logic from TRL 4 design - space definition to TRL 6 validation, through the achievement of the six main objectives. WP1 provides our project management, quality assurance, risk control, and formal gate governance across the full action. WP2 defines the constrained alloy design space and establishes our initial modelling basis through physics-informed priors, literature mining, and uncertainty-aware surrogate models. WP3 generates our combinatorial PVD thin-film decision libraries, while WP4 parallels this screening by mapping LPBF process windows, executing reuse cycling, and producing paired thin-film–bulk calibration datasets (paired composition–property measurements on the same alloys in both thin-film and bulk form, enabling our WP5 transferability model). WP5 converts validated evidence into candidate-selection rules through transferability calibration, multi-fidelity surrogate modelling, and constrained Bayesian optimisation. WP6 delivers our circular feedstock governance backbone, establishing protocols for the genealogy, qualification, and routing of powder batches for the three circular loops (LPBF reuse, PVD target consolidation, HVOF thermal-spray valorisation). WP7 delivers the FAIR digital backbone and platform MVP, including schema, provenance, and conformance validation, and WP8 builds on this foundation to deploy our AI-driven workflows, evidence packaging tools, and final open-core platform release with EU AI Act-compliant explainability. WP9 operates throughout the project as our cross-cutting SSbD, LCA, and circularity assessment layer. WP10 prepares our communication, dissemination, stakeholder engagement, KPI harmonisation, and uptake-readiness assets, while WP11 consolidates our standardisation contributions, including a CWA for circular HEA qualification, exploitation packaging, and final uptake-facing outputs. WP12 closes our workflow by building, qualifying, validating, and packaging the evidence for the three TRL 6 demonstrators. Our work-plan structure is intentionally sequential, where evidence must mature, and parallel, where enabling functions must remain continuously available. WP2, WP3, WP4, and WP7 establish our initial technical, experimental, and digital foundations. Their outputs feed WP5, which formalises decision rules for candidate narrowing and demonstrator-freeze choices. WP6 runs in parallel to ensure that circular feedstock routes are qualified as compositions mature. WP8 layers AI-driven workflow on the WP7 backbone from M9 and then executes the second-phase refinement, integration, and platform delivery needed to move from early evidence to route readiness. WP9 constrains our progression throughout by linking technical advancement to evidence of sustainability and circularity. WP10 supports uptake before demonstrator completion through stakeholder-facing and harmonisation activities, while WP11 takes over in the final phase to convert validated outcomes into standardisation-oriented and exploitation-ready outputs. WP12 is positioned at the end of our chain because it depends on frozen candidates, qualified routes, controlled evidence, and platform-supported traceability generated by the preceding work packages.

3.1 Work plan and resources Brief presentation of the overall structure of our work plan: Our 46-month H2SAVE work plan has been designed across 3 project stages (or phases), that are aligned with the Technology Readiness Level: Stage 1 (Subsystem Developments- TRL4), Stage 2 (System Integration & Prototype testing -TRL4-5), and Stage 3 (Operational Validation- TRL5). This structure also facilitates the implementation requirements of a Lump Sum funding model, in that our WPs are contained across three reporting periods (RP1: M1-M18; RP2: M19 -M36; RP3: M37 -M46), following the Horizon Europe Guide for Proposers for Lump Sum Grants50. As summarised below, and as illustrated in the Pert (Figure 6), this overall structure will ensure a coherent progression from materials and component development to integrated system validation, while maintaining strong cohesion between technological R&D, safety, digitalisation, and impact-oriented activities. Figure 3 presents the critical dependency logic of our H2SAVE work plan, showing the progression from materials and subsystem development (WP1–WP4), through prototype integration and Digital Twin/EMS calibration (WP5 –WP6), to representative operational validation and resilience assessment (WP7). The critical path is governed by milestone gates ML2, ML4, ML7 and ML9, which define the readiness conditions for progression across phases. In Phase 1 (M1–M18): We will focus on the development and validation of the core technologies at the subsystem level (TRL4), which includes materials and cell development (WP1), stack and hotbox design (WP2). It will also involve the establishment of safety, sustainability and techno-economic foundations in a ‘by-design’ approach (WP3), as well as defining the digital twin architecture and early control logic (WP4). These activities provide the scientific and engineering basis for subsequent system integration. In Phase 2 (M19 –M36): Our R&D work will advance towards system integration and prototype development (progressing from TRL4 to TRL5). In this phase, cell manufacturing, optimisation and validation will be carried out in WP5, followed by full system integration, hybrid energy management system (EMS) implementation, and digital twin calibration in WP6. This phase enables the transition from component-level validation to an integrated, functional energy storage system operating under representative conditions. In Phase 3 (M37–M46), we will focus on validation in a representative environment (to confirm that TRL5 has been reached). WP7 addresses integrated system testing under critical infrastructure scenarios, including dynamic operation and resilience-oriented validation. Final safety validation, life cycle and techno-economic assessments will be completed using operational data generated during this phase.
